title: Ajahn Funn: When the mind is not good, know it and think “Buddho, Buddho” – cut it off. Now, what about happiness and prosperity? Our…
content:
When the Mind Is Good, Everything Is Good – Happiness and Prosperity Follow

When the mind is not good, know it and think “Buddho, Buddho” – cut it off. Now, what about happiness and prosperity? Our mind is good, happy and at ease, cool-hearted, free from suffering, heat, turmoil, and trouble. This produces happiness and prosperity. When our mind is good, everything we do is good. Business is good, livelihood is good, study is good, whatever we do is good. Family is good, neighbors, marketplace, and the nation are good. It is like this. Let everyone come to know. When our mind is good and thinks good, we are happy. Sitting is happy, lying down is happy, walking is happy, standing is happy. The mind is happy, the mind is at ease. When the mind is at ease, everything you do is easy, everything you seek is easy, everything you take is easy, business is easy, government service is easy, family is easy, parents and siblings are easy, neighbors and marketplace are easy, the nation is easy. Look – what do you say, all of us?

title: Ajahn Funn: Rice, water, food – they don't tell us to eat them. Do they? Does rice say, "If you don't eat me, I'll hit…
content:
Rice and Money Are Neutral – We Are the Ones Who Want Them

Rice, water, food – they don't tell us to eat them. Do they? Does rice say, "If you don't eat me, I'll hit your mouth"? Rice is neutral, isn't it? Does money say, "If you don't keep me, I'll hit your hand"? It doesn't say anything. It remains neutral. Left in the sun and rain, it stays like that. That's how it is. But we want it ourselves. If we want it, then we must be diligent and industrious. But let the mind be happy, let the mind be at ease.

When the mind is happy and at ease, everything is at ease. That's the important point. Has your tiredness gone? If you want to sleep, sleep while focusing on the heart until you fall asleep. When the mind has Buddho, it is greater than everything. Greater than Indra, Brahma, devaputras, devas, yakkhas, kumbhāṇḍas, garuḍas, nāgas. When has Buddho ever been insignificant? Our mind is greater than all of them. That's how it is. Do you understand? Alright, we can stop.

title: Ajahn Funn: We have taken it to be a self, a being, a person, a me, a him. When we contemplate the basic kammaṭṭhāna and…
content:
The 32 Parts: Bile, Phlegm, Pus, Blood – Where Is the Person Here?

We have taken it to be a self, a being, a person, a me, a him. When we contemplate the basic kammaṭṭhāna and see that it is not a being, not a person, not a self, not a soul, not me, not him – why is that said? Let us contemplate. Raise it up like this: pittaṃ (bile), semhaṃ (phlegm), pubbo (pus), lohitaṃ (blood), sedo (sweat), medo (fat), assu (tears), vasā (oil), kheḷo (saliva), siṅghāṇikā (snot), lasikā (synovial fluid), muttaṃ (urine). Where is the person here? None of this is woman or man, no one at all. It is simply that.

When we see this, we can abandon sakkāyadiṭṭhi (personality belief). As for vicikicchā (doubt), the uncertainty whether it might be this or that – there is none. It is truly that way. Seeing that, our mind becomes empty, calms down. The matter is just like this. We can see it clearly. For example, how could snot be a person? How could saliva be a person? How could urine be a person? Contemplate and see. When we see clearly, truly, directly like that, we see that there is no self, no being, no person, no me, no him. Those things are not anything; they simply exist that way. We have been deluded, grasping them as a self, as a soul.

title: Ajahn Funn: The other extreme that you, as renunciants, should not pursue is hate — the fire of dosa. When the fire of dosa ignites…
content:
The Fire of Anger (Dosa) Also Burns – Avoid Both Extremes

The other extreme that you, as renunciants, should not pursue is hate — the fire of dosa. When the fire of dosa ignites in the mind-continuum (citta-santāna) of anyone, it burns that person, causing them to think maliciously and do evil, base deeds. They have no hiri and ottappa — no shame or fear of wrongdoing. That evil they have done brings suffering, harm, and danger, causing mental distress upon themselves. This is the result of the extreme that monks should not pursue — that is, hate. As for not loving and not hating — that is the majjhimāpaṭipadā, the middle way of practice that monks should all follow and observe. This middle way, this paṭipadā, is a path free from peril and danger. Just as when we travel, if we step to the left we encounter only thorns and brambles, and if we step to the right we also encounter thorns. Or like a car that leaves the road onto the left shoulder — it gets stuck and overturns; if it goes onto the right shoulder — it also falls and overturns, causing danger and damage to itself. If the car stays right on the road without veering off, it can travel comfortably and safely reach its destination.

title: Ajahn Funn: Note this well. Alright, today listen to the discourse. Have you had enough of listening to discourses? You all already know. You already…
content:
Buddho, Buddho – The Knower in the Heart, Our Refuge and Abode

Note this well.

Alright, today listen to the discourse. Have you had enough of listening to discourses? You all already know. You already know everything, don't you? Birth, aging, sickness, death you already know, don't you? Happiness and suffering you already know, don't you? Or not? We have seen it all. From now on, listen to Dhamma. Listening to Dhamma has no end. Come, take your place to listen to Dhamma. Sit comfortably, sit cross-legged with right leg over left, right hand over left if you like. When your mind is at ease, recollect your refuge. What is our refuge? The Buddha, Dhamma, Saṅgha. Believe that the Buddha is in the heart, the Dhamma is in the heart, the Noble Saṅgha is in the heart. Why say they are in the heart? Buddha — knowing — is in the heart, not anywhere else.

Believe firmly like that, then recite and meditate like this: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho, Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho, Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho three times. Then combine into just the single word Buddho, Buddho. Close your eyes, shut your mouth. The tongue does not move. Recollect within the heart. Where does the feeling of Buddho know? Recollect and see. Buddho — wherever it feels, set up mindfulness right there to watch that knowing.

title: Ajahn Funn: When our body is comfortable, recite or recollect: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho three times, then settle on just Buddho, Buddho as a single word.…
content:
Establish the Mind in Buddho: The Knower Is Not Subject to Destruction

When our body is comfortable, recite or recollect: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho three times, then settle on just Buddho, Buddho as a single word. Don't send your mind forward, backward, left, right, up, or down. Establish it right in the middle, at the place of knowing. Buddho, Buddho – stay there. Don't send your mind elsewhere. Close your eyes, close your mouth, and focus. Make the mind calm. We want happiness and comfort. Don't think about suffering and difficulty. Whatever is suffering – that is not our self. Don't send the mind out. Establish it at the place of knowing. This "knowing" is important. It is not subject to breaking, destruction, or loss. It is the inner knower. Focus to see that it exists individually for each person. Happiness and suffering, good and bad – all are conventions. Happiness is something we label ourselves. Suffering we label ourselves. Good we label ourselves. Bad we label ourselves. Don't label anything. Cool, hot – we label it all ourselves. Don't label. Just set the mind at ease. That's enough. Then things will be good.

title: Ajahn Funn: If our mind is calm and still, not reaching out forward or backward, left or right, above or below, but established right in…
content:
Establishing the Knower Within: Suffering Is Because of Delusion

If our mind is calm and still, not reaching out forward or backward, left or right, above or below, but established right in the middle of knowing — not conceiving of past or future, just determining and watching the present moment — sitting and watching right now: is it happy or suffering? Suffering is something we call suffering; happiness is something we call happiness. If we do not call it happiness, will it be there? We grasp at happiness, so it becomes happiness. We grasp at suffering, so it becomes suffering. Therefore, we must remain calm and still, not leaning here or there. Establish yourself in buddho — the knower within.

All of us wish to be free from suffering. Where is suffering? Suffering is not in trees, mountains, or jungles. This very heart of ours is suffering. Suffering because of delusion. Now, as for freedom from suffering: as we sit here, our mind is refreshed. Buddho — establishing the knower within — it does not suffer. When the mind does not suffer, that means it is free from suffering. Happiness because there is no suffering. This is nothing else.

Suffering is not because of clothes, not because of a piece of cloth, not because of a dwelling, not because of money or wealth, not because of relatives or siblings. Is it that parents and siblings cause suffering? That possessions and money cause suffering? That work causes suffering? Look: as we sit here, what is there? Sitting here right now, there is absolutely nothing, isn't that true? Where is any business? Where is any work? If we just sit still, why would there be suffering? That's the point: suffering is because our mind is deluded.

title: Ajahn Funn: When we know and see like this, we withdraw clinging to the aggregates. It is not a self, not a being, not a…
content:
When We See the Truth, We Withdraw Clinging to Aggregates and Let Go

When we know and see like this, we withdraw clinging to the aggregates. It is not a self, not a being, not a person, not beautiful, not handsome. Our mind can then let go. It abandons personality view. No matter what anyone says or curses, we don’t get angry. There’s nothing – why get angry? So the Buddha taught this to enable us to abandon greed, hatred, and delusion. By contemplating this mūla-kammaṭṭhāna, when we see the truth, we withdraw from becomings and births; we no longer wander on in birth and death. If we see this, we know Dhamma. When we know Dhamma and see Dhamma, our mind becomes steady and unshakable.

From now on, you must go to the temple every day to listen to Dhamma. Remember this. Our refuge is Buddho – the Buddha; Dhammo – the Dhamma; Saṅgho – the Noble Sangha. You must pay homage every day. Our refuges are only these – nothing else can be relied upon. Material possessions, money, wealth, buildings, houses – they cannot be relied upon. Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho – when condensed, become the single word Buddho. If you lack Buddha – that is, the knowing one – you cannot rely on anything at all. You cannot rely on Dhammo nor Saṅgho.

title: Ajahn Funn: Now, sit down. Sit in meditation. Sit comfortably. Arrange your posture and demeanor with dignity, with a bright, smiling face. When you have…
content:
Meditating on Buddho, Buddho – Watching the Knower in the Heart

Now, sit down. Sit in meditation. Sit comfortably. Arrange your posture and demeanor with dignity, with a bright, smiling face. When you have made the body comfortable, recollect our refuge. What is our refuge? The Buddha, the Dhamma, the Saṅgha – these are our refuge. Recollect the Buddha in your mind, the Dhamma in your mind, the noble Saṅgha disciples in your mind. Have confident faith in that way. Then recite the meditation phrase: Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho; Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho; Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho – three times. Then consolidate it into just Buddho, Buddho – one word. Close your eyes, close your mouth. Recollect it inwardly. Don't wiggle your tongue. We want to know what is lacking in ourselves – what is good, what is not good, so that we know. We want to know whether it is good or bad. What level are we on? In what realm? Why have we done something and not achieved our aspiration? For what reason? Look and see. How will we know? Recollect Buddho, Buddho – wherever awareness arises, set up mindfulness and look intently at that knowing. Let the ear listen there as well; let everything look there. Don't look elsewhere.

title: Ajahn Funn: This knowing is extremely important. We must develop it further, make it further, contemplate it further, determine it further. You must focus on…
content:
Whatever You Grasp, It Becomes That – The Teacher Taught Not to Grasp Anything

This knowing is extremely important. We must develop it further, make it further, contemplate it further, determine it further. You must focus on one thing only. When we determine the mind to be still on one thing, then all diseases are calmed and cease completely. Dangers cease completely. The defilements, craving, lust, greed, hatred, delusion cease by themselves. Because that mind is no longer anything – then who would become anything? Dangers and enmity cease by themselves. That one has no danger – where would danger come from? That one has no enmity – where would enmity come from? That one has no kamma – where would kamma come from? That one has no evil – where would evil come from? That one has no suffering – where would suffering come from? It is merely the knower. Make it know right now. See clearly, directly, with certainty. Knowing is not suffering, not danger, not enmity. Right now, if we grasp danger, it becomes danger. If we grasp enmity, it becomes enmity. If we grasp kamma, it becomes kamma. If we grasp suffering, it becomes suffering. Whatever you grasp, it becomes that.

Therefore, the Teacher taught not to grasp anything at all. He taught to release, to let go, to abandon. In the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, he said: "Cāgo paṭinissaggo mutti anālayo." Cāgo means relinquishment, or abandoning, letting go, releasing.
